sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन ।
मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि ॥ ४७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
karmaṇy evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana |
mā karma-phala-hetur bhūr mā te saṅgo 'stv akarmaṇi || 47 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape, body text byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs — single danda vs double danda)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.047 = MBh_06,024.047). Word-segmentation differs: GRETIL writes 'kadā cana' as two words; modern editions write 'kadācana'. Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: the cause of the fruits of action

translations.verbatim_quote: You have a right to perform your prescribed duty, but you are not entitled to the fruits of action. Never consider yourself the cause of the results of your activities, and never be attached to not doing your duty.

You have a right to perform your prescribed duties, but you are not entitled to the fruits of your actions. Never consider yourself to be the cause of the results of your activities, nor be attached to inaction.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt, 280 chars of 1552-char full purport)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There are three considerations here: prescribed duties, capricious work, and inaction. Prescribed duties are activities enjoined in terms of one's acquired modes of material nature. Capricious work means actions without the sanction of authority, and inaction means not performing one's prescribed duties.

commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The right pertains to action alone, not to steadfastness in Knowledge. Even when acting, one must not crave the results, because desire for fruits binds the actor as the agent of those results. Aversion to action is also rejected; one is to perform duty without becoming the agent of its fruit and without inclining toward inaction.

The aspirant established in sattva has a right to obligatory, occasional, and desiderative acts taught in the Veda, but no right to their fruits. Acts performed with fruit-desire create bondage; the same acts performed without such attachment become worship and enable liberation. Agency and the desire for results belong to the gunas or the Lord. Inaction (as Arjuna proposed by abandoning his warrior duty) is also rejected.

positions.rendering: right pertains to action, not Knowledge

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All translators render adhikāraḥ as 'right' or 'entitlement' but differ on its scope. Shankara reads it as a contrast with jnana-niṣṭhā (steadfastness in Knowledge) — the verse applies to those who act, not those who have realised non-doership. Ramanuja reads it as scoped to specific Vedic-prescribed action categories (obligatory, occasional, desiderative). Prabhupada and Mukundananda flatten this nuance into 'prescribed duty', a phrasing general enough to be uncontroversial across traditions.

literal_meaning: Your right is to action alone, never to its fruits. Do not be the cause of action's fruit, nor be attached to inaction.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna establishes the doctrine of niṣkāma-karma — action without attachment to its results — as the resolution of Arjuna's paralysis. The actor's only legitimate domain is the action itself. The fruits are governed by impersonal forces (gunas, the Lord, dharma) outside the actor's control. Importantly, this is not a license for inaction; renunciation of action altogether is also rejected. The middle path is engaged action plus released outcome.

remaining_gaps: Shankara's bhashya verbatim — currently summary-only because bhagavad-gita.us renders commentary in JS
GRETIL critical edition not yet pulled (not strictly needed since 4 sources agree on body text)
